title: Happy Place
author: Emily Henry
prompt:
Question: Which of the following passages is verbatim from the "Happy Place" book by Emily Henry?
Options:
A. A bliss so brilliant and searing you sense it could incinerate you. That comes after that night, when Wyn goes out for Chinese food, comes back drenched by the rain. At the sound of the door shutting, I look up from the crossword I've gone back to, get up from the couch, and walk over to help him with the rain-flecked bags. I turn on the stove to make tea and take the bags from his arms, and as I'm putting them on the counter, he grabs my wrist and looks down at me with such tenderness and defenselessness that I'm afraid, sure something terrible has happened.
B. A happiness so bright and hot you feel like it could incinerate you. That comes later that night, when Wyn goes out to pick up Chinese food, comes back drenched from the rain. At the sound of the door clicking shut, I look up from the crossword Ive gone back to, jump up from the couch, and pad over to help him with the rain-speckled paper bags. I flick the stove on to make tea and take the bags from his arms, and as Im setting them on the counter, he catches me by the wrist and looks down at me with such softness and vulnerability that Im afraid, sure something terrible has happened.
C. A joy so intense and burning you feel it might consume you. That arrives later that evening, when Wyn exits to get Chinese takeout, returning soaked by the downpour. When I hear the door close, I glance up from the crossword I've returned to, rise from the sofa, and shuffle over to assist with the rain-dotted sacks. I ignite the stove to boil tea and take the bags from his grasp, and as I place them on the counter, he seizes my wrist and gazes down at me with such gentleness and openness that I'm scared, certain something awful has transpired.
D. A felicity so intense and fiery you feel it may immolate you. That arrives subsequent that evening, when Wyn departs to procure Chinese cuisine, returning saturated from the downpour. Upon hearing the door close, I glance up from the crossword I've returned to, arise from the sofa, and saunter over to assist with the rain-speckled parcels. I ignite the stove to brew tea and take the bags from his grasp, and as I situate them on the counter, he seizes my wrist and gazes down at me with such affection and vulnerability that I'm frightened, certain something dire has transpired.
Answer:
